goal: Make GPU behavior visible using CUDA, Nsight, and PyTorch benchmarks.

tasks.title: Verify CUDA environment
tasks.objective: Confirm that NVIDIA driver, CUDA compiler, and PyTorch CUDA are working.
tasks.deliverable: Create hardware.md with GPU, driver, CUDA, PyTorch, CPU, RAM, and OS.

command_prompts.question: What does each of the 3 commands check, in order?

expected_answers.response: Right - nvidia-smi shows the GPU model, driver version, and current utilization.
Right - nvcc --version shows the CUDA compiler version installed.
Right - the Python torch check verifies that PyTorch sees CUDA and can name the GPU. If it doesn't, PyTorch was built without CUDA support.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the 3 commands check 3 different layers (driver, compiler, Python binding). Each can fail independently.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - driver (nvidia-smi), compiler (nvcc), Python binding (torch). Three independent layers.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: 3 layers. nvidia-smi = driver. nvcc = compiler. torch = Python binding. Each can be missing or wrong.

tasks.title: Run CUDA deviceQuery
tasks.objective: Inspect GPU hardware limits.
tasks.deliverable: Record compute capability, SM count, warp size, max threads per block, and global memory.

command_prompts.question: What 3 device properties does deviceQuery report that you'll need later?

expected_answers.response: Right - SM (streaming multiprocessor) count determines max parallelism and occupancy.
Right - global memory size and bandwidth set the upper bound for memory-bound kernel speed.
Right - compute capability (e.g. 8.0, 8.6) determines which PTX/SASS features you can use.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - clock speed is a single number, not 3 properties. The 3 properties you actually use later are SM count, memory size, and compute capability.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - SM count, global memory, compute capability. These are the 3 you cite in every later task.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: the 3 you cite later are SM count (parallelism), global memory (size + bandwidth), compute capability (feature support).

tasks.title: Run CUDA bandwidthTest
tasks.objective: Measure practical host-device and device memory bandwidth.
tasks.deliverable: Record measured memory bandwidth and compare it to theoretical GPU bandwidth if available.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why is bandwidthTest's main number device-to-device bandwidth, not host-to-device?

expected_answers.response: PCIe matters at transfer time, but bandwidthTest reports the sustained device-memory number, which is what limits in-kernel data movement.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the device-memory bus is the bottleneck bandwidthTest measures, not the SM compute. The number is bytes-per-second between HBM and SMs, not PCIe.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - the device-memory bus is what limits in-kernel data movement; PCIe only shows up at transfer time.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: bandwidthTest measures device-to-device bandwidth (HBM <-> SMs). PCIe shows up at host<->device transfer, not in steady-state kernels.

tasks.title: Write first CUDA vector add kernel
tasks.objective: Implement C[i] = A[i] + B[i] and test different vector sizes/block sizes.
tasks.deliverable: Working kernel in labs/vector_add/vector_add.cu and a short note on achieved bandwidth.
tasks.reality_check: Why is vector add usually memory-bound? Answer in 2 sentences.

teaching_prompts.question: Why is vector add usually memory-bound? Answer in 2 sentences.

expected_answers.response: Right - the bottleneck is bytes-per-flop. Vector add does ~1 FLOP per element but 2 memory accesses, so it spends most of its time waiting on the GPU's memory subsystem, not the compute units.
Occupancy and launch overhead matter, but the dominant reason vector add is memory-bound is bytes-per-flop. Compute utilization is typically <10% on a real card.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the GPU has many cores, but vector add does not use them. Each element does ~1 FLOP, so even with thousands of SMs running, the bottleneck is the memory bus, not compute.

teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good. Now: how would you change the kernel to be compute-bound instead? (one sentence is fine)
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about the ratio of bytes read+written to FLOPs performed. A 1:1 element-wise op usually does very few FLOPs per element.

teaching_prompts.question: How would you make vector add faster without changing the algorithm?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Right. Vectorized loads + higher occupancy is the standard answer; the wall-clock win is usually 2-4x on a memory-bound kernel.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Two common answers: (a) load 4 floats at a time with float4 / vector loads, (b) increase occupancy so more in-flight loads hide the latency.

tasks.title: Profile with Nsight Systems
tasks.objective: Capture a timeline of the vector add kernel and identify gaps.
tasks.deliverable: Save the .nsys-rep and note kernel time vs total time.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why does nsys show 'kernels' as the top time spender on a memory-bound kernel?

expected_answers.response: Right - 'kernels' time is the time the SMs are *busy*, including waiting on memory. A memory-bound kernel keeps the SMs busy stalling on loads, not doing FLOPs.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - 'kernels' time is wall-clock spent in the kernel, not FLOP throughput. On a memory-bound kernel, the SMs are busy stalling on loads, not executing instructions.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - 'kernels' is wall-clock in the kernel. On memory-bound code, most of that is stalls.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: 'kernels' is total time in the kernel. A memory-bound kernel still has the SMs 'busy' - just stalling on loads, not executing FLOPs.

tasks.title: Profile with Nsight Compute
tasks.objective: Get per-kernel metrics: SM occupancy, memory throughput, achieved bandwidth.
tasks.deliverable: Save ncu report and note achieved memory bandwidth vs roofline.
tasks.reality_check: What is the SOL (speed-of-light) percentage, and what does it tell you?

teaching_prompts.question: What does 'SOL' mean in ncu's Speed Of Light report, and why does it matter for kernel optimization?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Right. SOL is a roofline-style metric: how close this kernel is to the theoretical peak. Sub-50% usually means memory bound; >70% means you're near the ceiling and need a different algorithm.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: SOL = Speed Of Light. It's a percent, against the GPU's theoretical peak for that resource (memory bandwidth, SM throughput, etc.). A 100% SOL means the kernel is at the ceiling.

teaching_prompts.question: If SOL is 30% and the bottleneck says 'memory_bound', what's the first thing you'd try?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Yes. Either get more bytes per thread (vectorized loads) or get more in-flight loads (occupancy / shared-memory tiling) so the SMs are fed. Both at once is the typical CUDA optimization play.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Two common answers: (a) use vectorized loads (float4 / int4) to widen the memory access, (b) increase occupancy so more warps are in flight hiding the latency.

tasks.title: Run PyTorch matmul benchmark
tasks.objective: Benchmark matmul at multiple sizes and dtypes; see where TFLOPS plateau.
tasks.deliverable: Note TFLOPS at 1024, 4096, 8192 and the dtype that wins.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why is a 4096x4096 matmul closer to compute-bound than a 1024x1024?

expected_answers.response: Arithmetic intensity scales with matrix size; small matrices don't reuse cache lines enough to feed the tensor cores, so memory bandwidth dominates.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - size matters because of arithmetic intensity, not just total FLOPs. A 1024x1024 has too little reuse per byte loaded to keep the tensor cores fed.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - bigger matrices mean more reuse per byte loaded, which raises arithmetic intensity past the ridge point.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about how many FLOPs happen per byte loaded. Small matrices don't reuse cache lines enough to feed the tensor cores.

tasks.title: Write Week 1 report
tasks.objective: Synthesize all observations into gpu-week1-report.md.
tasks.deliverable: Complete gpu-week1-report.md with: hardware, key observations, bottlenecks found, and next steps.

deliverable_prompts.question: What are the 4 sections your gpu-week1-report.md needs to be complete?

expected_answers.response: Right - the hardware section needs GPU model, driver version, CUDA version, PyTorch CUDA availability, and OS.
Right - 'key observations' is the place for the actual numbers you measured (bandwidth, kernel time, occupancy), not generic statements.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the deliverable is a structured report, not a summary. Each of the 4 sections needs concrete numbers from your runs.

deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good. The 4 sections are: hardware, key observations, bottlenecks found, next steps.
deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: the deliverable lists 4 sections - hardware, key observations, bottlenecks found, and next steps. Name them.

tasks.title: Install Triton and run a sanity check
tasks.objective: Install Triton in a Python venv and run the bundled tutorial matmul kernel. Verify you can launch a Triton kernel on the available GPU.
commands: python -c "import triton; import triton.language as tl; print(triton.__version__)"
tasks.deliverable: notes/triton_setup.md with the installed Triton version, Python/PyTorch versions, and the GPU it ran on (or 'no NVIDIA GPU - documented what would have run').

command_prompts.question: What does the 3-command sequence do?

expected_answers.response: Right - the venv creates an isolated Python environment so Triton doesn't fight with your other projects.
Right - pip install pulls in Triton + PyTorch, which Triton depends on for tensor manipulation.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the venv-then-pip-then-verify pattern is the standard way to avoid version conflicts with other Python projects. It's not optional.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - venv (isolation), pip install (deps), verify (print version).
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: venv isolates, pip installs, the print line verifies. Three different purposes.

tasks.title: First Triton kernel: vector add
tasks.objective: Re-implement the Week 1 vector add as a Triton kernel. Compare line count and readability vs the raw CUDA version.
tasks.deliverable: labs/vector_add/triton_vadd.py plus a 3-line note in reports/triton_vs_cuda.md comparing the two implementations.

command_prompts.question: What 2-3 things does a Triton kernel need that a CUDA kernel doesn't?

expected_answers.response: Right - Triton kernels take a BLOCK_SIZE parameter (and often an autotune config) that's analogous to launch params in CUDA.
Right - Triton uses tl.load / tl.store with cache modifiers instead of raw pointer arithmetic.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - Triton is not a drop-in CUDA replacement. You write a Python kernel with a JIT-compiled inner function. Different mental model.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - launch params (BLOCK_SIZE, grid), tl.load/tl.store memory hints, and (often) autotune configs.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: Triton is Python + JIT. You pass BLOCK_SIZE / grid at launch, you use tl.load/tl.store instead of pointer math.

tasks.title: Triton matmul with autotune
tasks.objective: Implement a block-tiled matmul in Triton and let autotune pick the best (BLOCK_M, BLOCK_N, BLOCK_K, num_stages) for your GPU.
tasks.deliverable: labs/triton_matmul/matmul.py and a note with the autotuned config + measured TFLOPS at one problem size.

command_prompts.question: Why is BLOCK_K (the K-dimension tile size) usually the autotune knob that matters most for matmul?

expected_answers.response: Right - BLOCK_K controls how many K-iterations share a tile of A and B. Bigger K = more reuse per byte loaded.
Right - too small K and you thrash the cache; too large and you spill registers. The sweet spot is bandwidth-bound.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - bigger BLOCK_K is better until it spills registers or exceeds shared memory. The autotune finds the sweet spot.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - BLOCK_K controls A/B reuse per K-step. Autotune finds the sweet spot between cache hits and register pressure.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: BLOCK_K controls how many K iterations reuse a tile of A and B. Bigger = more reuse, until register pressure or shared memory caps it.

tasks.title: Fused softmax: one kernel vs three
tasks.objective: Write a single-pass online softmax in Triton. Compare it to a naive three-pass softmax (max, subtract, exp+normalize) on a large row.
tasks.deliverable: labs/triton_softmax/softmax.py + a 5-line note on achieved bandwidth improvement.

command_prompts.question: What does 'fused' save you compared to running 3 separate kernels?

expected_answers.response: Right - 3 separate kernels each round-trip the full tensor through global memory. Fused = one round-trip.
Right - softmax is memory-bound on the read+write of the full tensor. Removing 2 of the 3 round-trips saves 2x bandwidth.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - softmax is memory-bound, not compute-bound. The win is from removing 2 memory round-trips, not from saving FLOPs.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - fused softmax saves 2 memory round-trips. It's a memory-bandwidth win, not a FLOP win.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: 3 separate kernels = 3 round-trips through global memory. Fused = 1 round-trip. The win is bandwidth, not FLOPs.

tasks.title: Flash-attention-style kernel draft
tasks.objective: Draft a flash-attention-style block-tiled attention kernel. Goal is structure, not SOTA. Read the FlashAttention paper for the tiling idea.
tasks.deliverable: labs/triton_attention/attention.py (working or partially working) + a 1-paragraph note on what the FlashAttention paper does that a naive attention does not.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why is attention memory-bound for long sequences?

expected_answers.response: Each token reads/writes the full KV cache every step; the FLOPs are O(N*d^2) but the memory is O(N^2*d), which dominates past N ~= d.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - FlashAttention changes the constant but not the asymptotic story; for long N, you still touch O(N^2) memory in the K/V loads.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - per-step memory grows as O(N*d) per token, so total memory is O(N^2*d), which outpaces the O(N*d^2) FLOPs when N is large.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about what each generated token has to read. The KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, so per-step memory grows too.

tasks.title: Triton vs raw CUDA synthesis
tasks.objective: Compare the Triton kernels from this week to the raw CUDA kernels from Week 1. Same workload, same machine. Note where Triton wins and where it loses.
tasks.deliverable: reports/triton_vs_cuda.md with a table of (kernel, lines-of-code, achieved perf, complexity of writing) for the vector_add and matmul comparisons.

deliverable_prompts.question: What 2-3 numbers belong in the 'speedup vs raw CUDA' section of your Triton synthesis?

expected_answers.response: Right - the 3 Triton kernels (vector add, matmul, fused softmax) each get a row in the comparison.
Right - the numbers are kernel time (ms) and achieved bandwidth (GB/s) for both the Triton and raw CUDA versions, per kernel.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - Triton isn't always faster. Fused softmax is usually a clear win; vector add is often similar to raw CUDA; matmul depends on the autotune config.

deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - 3 kernels, 2 versions each, 2 metrics each. That's the comparison table.
deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: there are 3 Triton kernels (vector add, matmul, fused softmax). For each, you compare against raw CUDA on 2 metrics: kernel time and achieved bandwidth.

tasks.title: Set up Python + transformers + a small model
tasks.objective: Install transformers + a small open model (e.g. Qwen2.5-0.5B or SmolLM2-360M) and verify you can load and run a forward pass.
commands: python -c "from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer; m = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B'); print(sum(p.numel() for p in m.parameters())/1e6, 'M params')"
tasks.deliverable: notes/llm_env.md with the model name, parameter count, device used (CPU / MPS / CUDA), and one sentence on what the model loaded successfully.

command_prompts.question: What 2 things do you need to verify after the install?

expected_answers.response: Right - verify transformers imports and a small model loads. The .from_pretrained call also downloads and caches weights.
Right - verify the model lands on CUDA. A model that loads to CPU is 'working' but not 'GPU-working'.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - 'imports' is not the same as 'works on GPU'. The .to('cuda') call is the actual proof.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - import works, model lands on CUDA. Both checks are needed.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: 2 checks. (1) transformers + a small model loads. (2) The model lands on the GPU, not the CPU.

tasks.title: First generation: measure latency
tasks.objective: Run a single text generation, measure time-to-first-token (TTFT) and inter-token latency. Note the device and batch size.
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/first_gen.py + a 1-line note: 'TTFT=Xms, inter-token=Yms, device=Z'.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why is 'time to first token' a memory-bound metric, not compute-bound?

expected_answers.response: Prefill is compute-bound (parallel), but TTFT includes the first decode step, which is a single-token memory-bound lookup against the freshly-built KV cache.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - prefill is the slow part, but it's compute-bound and parallel. TTFT specifically measures the wall-clock to the first *output* token, which depends on the first decode step.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - TTFT = prefill + first-decode. The first decode is memory-bound (one new token, full KV read).
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: TTFT = prefill time + first decode step. Prefill is compute-bound (parallel); the first decode is memory-bound (one token, full KV read).

tasks.title: Profile prefill vs decode and watch KV cache grow
tasks.objective: Separate the prefill phase (processing the prompt) from the decode phase (one token at a time). Confirm the wall-clock asymmetry and watch the KV cache size scale with sequence length.
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/prefill_decode.py + a short note: 'prefill=Xms for N prompt tokens, decode=Yms per token, KV cache=ZMB at seq_len=W'.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why does decode-step latency stay roughly constant as the prompt grows, but prefill latency scales linearly?

expected_answers.response: Decode does O(1) work per step (one new token, one KV-cache append); prefill does O(N) work to process the full prompt.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the KV cache does grow, but per-step decode work is O(1) in N. The cache read is one new row of K and V, not a scan of the full cache.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - per-step decode work is O(1) in sequence length; only prefill scales with N.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: what does each decode step actually *do*? It's one new token, one KV append. Prefill processes the whole prompt, so it scales with N.

tasks.title: Batched generation: throughput vs latency tradeoff
tasks.objective: Send B=1, 4, 16, 64 sequences through the model in a single batch. Plot tokens/sec vs batch size and per-request latency.
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/batch_sweep.py + a 3-line note on where throughput stops scaling (memory-bound? SM-bound?).
tasks.reality_check: At what batch size does throughput stop scaling? Why?

teaching_prompts.question: At what batch size does LLM throughput stop scaling? Why?

expected_answers.response: Right - the dominant ceiling is KV-cache memory, not compute. At small batches the GPU is underutilized; throughput grows. Past a point, KV-cache growth per sequence fills HBM and you have to either truncate, evict, or paged-attend. The compute-vs-memory crossover is batch-specific.
Compute saturation does happen, but it is usually the second ceiling, not the first. The first wall is KV-cache memory, not arithmetic throughput.

common_misconceptions.response: Sequence length matters but it is not the throughput ceiling per se. What hurts at large batch is per-sequence KV-cache growth compounding with batch size. Prompt length only sets the starting KV size, not the per-step growth rate.

teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good. How would you measure where the crossover is in practice (which number on which dashboard)?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about what scales linearly with batch size, and what stays fixed. The thing that scales is what hurts first.

teaching_prompts.question: Pick one: a system that hits 'compute bound' at batch=8, vs one that hits 'memory bound' at batch=8. Which is easier to scale further, and why?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Right. Memory-bound is usually easier to scale because you have more knobs (KV eviction, paged attention, int8/int4 quant of KV, sequence parallelism). Compute-bound at small batch is often a sign of an under-optimized kernel, not a real ceiling.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about which has more 'knobs' to turn. Memory pressure has a stack of techniques (paging, eviction, quantization). Compute pressure at batch=8 is usually fixable with a better kernel, not a fundamental limit.

tasks.title: Int8 quantize a small model
tasks.objective: Apply int8 weight quantization (bitsandbytes or torchao) to the same small model. Measure latency, peak memory, and a tiny qualitative check (a few prompts, eyeball the output).
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/int8_quant.py + a 4-line note: latency delta, memory delta, qualitative output (pass/fail/almost).

command_prompts.question: What does bitsandbytes' int8 quantization actually change about the model?

expected_answers.response: Right - the linear layer weights are stored as int8. The forward pass dequants on-the-fly during the matmul.
Right - the 4x weight-size reduction cuts the memory bandwidth needed to load the model, which speeds up decode.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - int8 doesn't make the compute faster; the dequant is added work. The win is memory bandwidth, which dominates decode.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - int8 quantizes the linear-layer weights. Win is memory bandwidth on decode, not compute speed.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: only the linear-layer weights are int8. The forward pass dequants on the fly. The win is weight-load bandwidth, not FLOPs.

tasks.title: LLM inference synthesis
tasks.objective: Synthesize this week's measurements into one report. Latency, memory, throughput, and what the bottleneck was at each scale.
tasks.deliverable: reports/llm_inference_week3.md with: prefill vs decode timing, KV cache growth curve, batch-sweep throughput curve, int8 quant tradeoffs.

deliverable_prompts.question: What 4 sections does your LLM inference synthesis need?

expected_answers.response: Right - 'first generation' covers the TTFT measurement and what dominated the wall-clock (prefill vs decode vs memory).
Right - 'KV cache' covers the profile of prefill vs decode and how the cache grows with sequence length.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the synthesis is about runtime behavior (TTFT, decode latency, KV growth, serving patterns), not model architecture choices.

deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - 4 sections: first generation, KV cache profile, decoding, serving patterns.
deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: the 4 sections mirror the 4 inference tasks - first generation, KV cache profile, decoding, and serving patterns.

tasks.title: Install vLLM (or TGI) and bring up a server
tasks.objective: Install a modern LLM serving engine (vLLM is the default pick). Start the OpenAI-compatible HTTP server and verify it responds.
tasks.deliverable: notes/serving_setup.md with the serving engine, model, port, and the curl command you used to verify it.

command_prompts.question: What 2 things do you need to verify after the vLLM install?

expected_answers.response: Right - bring up the server (default port 8000) and curl /v1/models to confirm it's listening.
Right - send a test completion (OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions) to confirm the model is actually loaded and serving tokens.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - 'imports cleanly' is not the same as 'serves tokens'. vLLM needs the model loaded and the server bound to a port.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - server up + curl /v1/chat/completions returns tokens. Two different checks.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: 2 checks. (1) Server is up on the port. (2) A test completion returns generated tokens (not an error).

tasks.title: First HTTP request: TTFT under serving
tasks.objective: Send a single HTTP request to the server. Measure TTFT and TPOT under serving. Compare to the raw transformers latency from Week 3.
tasks.deliverable: notes/serving_first_request.md with TTFT/TPOT vs the Week 3 numbers. Was vLLM faster? By how much?
tasks.reality_check: Why does vLLM beat raw transformers on throughput even for a single request? (Hint: PagedAttention + continuous batching.)

teaching_prompts.question: Why does vLLM beat raw transformers on throughput even for a single request?

expected_answers.response: Right - PagedAttention is the headline. It stores KV in fixed-size blocks (like virtual memory pages), so the allocator can pack sequences tightly without reserving worst-case contiguous memory. That means you fit more concurrent sequences in the same HBM, so even single-request steady-state is more efficient because the scheduler keeps the GPU fed between requests.

Continuous batching is half the answer. vLLM's scheduler swaps finished sequences out and new ones in at every decode step, so the GPU is never idle waiting for a batch to fill. PagedAttention is the other half - they need each other to work well.

common_misconceptions.response: Faster kernels matter but they are not what makes vLLM beat raw transformers on a single request. The win is in the scheduler + memory allocator, not the matmul speed.

teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good. What changes if you disable PagedAttention but keep continuous batching?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: it is not a faster kernel. It is how the system manages memory and schedules work between requests.

teaching_prompts.question: What changes if you disable PagedAttention but keep continuous batching?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Right. Without PagedAttention, KV is reserved worst-case contiguous, so the scheduler can only fit a few sequences. Continuous batching still swaps requests, but the per-sequence memory overhead is much higher, so concurrency drops and you are back near raw-transformer throughput.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: continuous batching without PagedAttention means each sequence still reserves worst-case contiguous KV. The scheduler can swap, but the per-sequence memory cost is much higher, so concurrency drops.

tasks.title: Read the PagedAttention paper and the vLLM source
tasks.objective: Read the vLLM paper (or the Kwon et al. SOSP'23 paper). Skim the vLLM source for the KV cache manager. Write 5 bullets connecting the paper to the code.
tasks.deliverable: notes/paged_attention_notes.md - 5 bullets, each with one paper claim and one code location that backs it.

command_prompts.question: What problem does PagedAttention solve that raw-transformers KV-cache management has?

expected_answers.response: Right - raw transformers over-reserves a contiguous KV cache per request (worst-case length). Most requests don't hit that length, so memory is wasted.
Right - PagedAttention stores the KV cache in non-contiguous blocks (pages), like OS virtual memory. Requests only pay for what they use.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - PagedAttention isn't a kernel optimization. It's a memory-management technique. FlashAttention is a separate (and complementary) win.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - PagedAttention fixes the per-request KV-cache over-reservation by storing in non-contiguous pages.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: it's about memory management, not kernel speed. Raw transformers waste memory by over-reserving contiguous KV per request.

tasks.title: Streaming responses: TTFT vs TPOT tradeoff
tasks.objective: Switch the request to stream=true. Measure TTFT (time to first SSE chunk) and TPOT (inter-chunk time). Note the difference.
tasks.deliverable: notes/serving_streaming.md with TTFT and TPOT, and a 2-line note on why streaming improves perceived latency without reducing total time-to-last-token.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why does streaming responses (sending tokens as they're decoded) reduce TTFT but not TPOT?

expected_answers.response: TTFT is wall-clock until the first byte hits the wire; streaming overlaps network with decode. TPOT is per-token-decode time, which is unchanged.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - streaming doesn't change decode speed; it changes when the *client* sees the first byte. TPOT measures decode-side latency, which is the same with or without streaming.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - TTFT is wire-side, TPOT is decode-side. Streaming overlaps network with decode, so it shrinks TTFT but not TPOT.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: TTFT measures the time until the *client* sees the first byte. TPOT measures per-token decode time. Streaming changes the first, not the second.

tasks.title: Concurrent requests: watch continuous batching kick in
tasks.objective: Send N=1, 4, 16, 64 concurrent requests with a small load tool. Watch throughput scale and per-request latency degrade. Confirm vLLM is continuous-batching (vs naive static batching).
tasks.deliverable: labs/serving_load/loadtest.py + a note: 'at N=X concurrent requests, aggregate throughput peaked at Y tok/s, per-request p99 latency was Zms'.

command_prompts.question: What's the 'continuous batching' win over static batching?

expected_answers.response: Right - in static batching, the next batch doesn't start until the slowest request finishes. Short requests sit idle waiting.
Right - continuous batching swaps finished requests out mid-step and slots new ones in, so the GPU is always busy.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - the throughput win comes from the GPU being always busy, not from 'more requests'. The mechanism is the swap-in / swap-out at each step.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - continuous batching swaps finished requests out mid-step so the GPU is always busy. Static batching makes short requests wait for the slowest.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: in static batching, the whole batch waits for the slowest request. Continuous batching swaps in new requests at every step.

tasks.title: GPU serving synthesis
tasks.objective: Write the systems report: TTFT vs TPOT, streaming vs non-streaming, continuous batching at load, what the bottleneck was at each scale.
tasks.deliverable: reports/gpu_serving_week4.md with: latency curves, throughput curves, a paragraph on what PagedAttention buys you, and one sentence on what would break at 1000 concurrent requests.

deliverable_prompts.question: What 3 things distinguish a vLLM-served deployment from a raw-transformers deployment?

expected_answers.response: Right - PagedAttention is the vLLM-specific innovation; it eliminates the per-request KV-cache reservation.
Right - continuous batching lets vLLM add new requests to a running batch instead of waiting for the full batch to finish.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - those optimizations exist in raw transformers too. vLLM's wins are PagedAttention + continuous batching, not kernels.

deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - PagedAttention, continuous batching, and (usually) tensor parallelism are the 3 distinguishing features.
deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: the 3 vLLM-specific features are PagedAttention, continuous batching, and (optionally) tensor parallelism. Raw transformers has none of these.

tasks.title: Quantize a teacher model to int8 (PTQ) and measure the gap
tasks.objective: Take a small open model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / SmolLM2-360M), apply post-training int8 quantization (bitsandbytes or torchao), and measure latency, peak memory, and perplexity against the fp16 baseline. Quantize-aware-distillation is later; this task is just the baseline.
tasks.deliverable: notes/distill_quant_int8.md with the fp16 vs int8 latency, peak memory, and a 1-paragraph note on where the gap shows up in perplexity.
commands: python -c "from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM; m = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B', load_in_8bit=True, device_map='auto'); print(m.get_memory_footprint()/1e9, 'GB')"

command_prompts.question: What does PTQ (post-training quantization) preserve vs change?

expected_answers.response: Right - PTQ changes only the weight storage (fp16 -> int8). The forward pass is the same shape; just with dequant on the fly.
Right - PTQ is a single calibration pass on a small dataset. No gradient updates, no retraining. The model is frozen.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - that's QAT (quantization-aware training). PTQ is one calibration pass + freeze, no gradients.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - PTQ changes only the weight storage, with a single calibration pass. No gradients.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: PTQ = one calibration pass on a small dataset + freeze. No retraining. If there's retraining, it's QAT (a different technique).

tasks.title: LoRA fine-tune a small model on a domain corpus
tasks.objective: LoRA-fine-tune Qwen2.5-0.5B on a small domain corpus (e.g. 5k samples from a public dataset) using PEFT. Compare adapter weights to a full-fine-tune baseline in parameter count only.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_lora_report.md with adapter rank, target modules, training loss curve description, and a 1-paragraph note on what the adapter learned.
commands: python -c "from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model; print('peft ready')"

command_prompts.question: Why is LoRA 'cheap' compared to full fine-tuning?

expected_answers.response: Right - LoRA adds a low-rank adapter (rank ~4-64) per linear layer, instead of updating the full weight matrix.
Right - a 7B model with LoRA r=16 trains <1% of the parameters. The base weights are frozen.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - LoRA is cheap per step, but the quality ceiling is lower than full fine-tuning for big domain shifts. It's a trade, not a free lunch.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - LoRA adds a small low-rank adapter per layer. The base weights are frozen. <1% trainable params for a 7B model.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: 'low-rank' = small adapter matrix per linear layer. Base weights frozen. Cheap = few trainable params, not 'free quality'.

tasks.title: Train a student model to match a teacher (logit KD)
tasks.objective: Train a small student (e.g. SmolLM2-135M) to mimic a larger teacher (Qwen2.5-0.5B) by matching output logits. Use temperature-scaled KL divergence as the loss; freeze the teacher; train the student for a few hundred steps on a domain corpus.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_kd_report.md with loss curve, temperature, alpha, and a 1-paragraph note on where the student catches up to the teacher.

command_prompts.question: What is the student trying to match in logit-KD (vs output-KD)?

expected_answers.response: Right - logit-KD matches the teacher's full output distribution (the soft probabilities, including the 'almost-right' tokens).
Right - output-KD matches just the argmax (the hard label). Logit-KD is richer because it includes the teacher's uncertainty.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - logit-KD carries the teacher's uncertainty ('the next token is 60% probably A but also 30% B and 10% C'). Output-KD loses that signal.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - logit-KD matches the full soft distribution. Output-KD only matches the argmax. The soft signal is the rich part.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: logit-KD = match the teacher's full output distribution (the soft probs). Output-KD = match just the top token. The soft signal is the teacher's uncertainty.

tasks.title: Evaluate teacher vs student vs LoRA vs int8 on a held-out task
tasks.objective: Build a small evaluation harness (perplexity on a held-out set, plus one downstream task from lm-evaluation-harness) and run it against the fp16 teacher, the int8 teacher, the LoRA-tuned teacher, and the distilled student. The point is the comparison, not the absolute number.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_eval.md with a 4-row table (model, perplexity, downstream task score, peak memory) and 2-3 sentences on the trade-offs.

bottleneck_followup.question: Why might the int8 student match the teacher on accuracy but lose 2x on throughput?

expected_answers.response: Int8 cuts memory bandwidth and weight loads, but dequant + atypical tensor shapes often fall off the optimized kernel paths.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - int8 reduces memory, but the throughput win assumes the kernel path stays optimized. If shapes are unusual, the kernel falls back to a slower path and dequant overhead eats the savings.

bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_match: Right - int8 helps when the kernel path stays optimized. Atypical shapes can drop you into a slower path where dequant overhead eats the savings.
bottleneck_followup.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: int8 reduces memory bandwidth, but the throughput win assumes an optimized kernel. If shapes are unusual, dequant overhead can eat the savings.

tasks.title: Int4 quantize the distilled student (GPTQ or AWQ)
tasks.objective: Take the distilled student from the prior task and quantize it to int4 using GPTQ or AWQ. Measure latency, memory, and a tiny perplexity check. Compare against the int8 baseline.
tasks.deliverable: notes/distill_int4.md with method (GPTQ / AWQ), latency, memory, perplexity, and a 1-paragraph note on where int4 starts to hurt.
commands: pip install -U auto-gptq   # or: pip install -U awq

command_prompts.question: What does int4 buy you that int8 doesn't?

expected_answers.response: Right - int4 halves the weight storage again. A 7B model in int4 is ~3.5GB instead of ~7GB.
Right - the smaller weight footprint lets the GPU load weights faster, which matters more on long-context decode where the model is the bandwidth ceiling.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - int4 has noticeable quality loss on most models. It's a 'size matters more than 1% accuracy' trade.

command_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - int4 halves the weight size again. Useful when memory is the ceiling, but you trade some accuracy.
command_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: int4 = half the int8 weight size. Useful when model size / memory bandwidth is the ceiling. Not free on accuracy.

tasks.title: Distillation track synthesis: when does each method pay off?
tasks.objective: Write the synthesis for the distillation track. Pull together the int8 baseline, the LoRA fine-tune, the teacher-student training, the eval comparison, and the int4 quant into a single report. The point is the trade-off map: when does LoRA win, when does distillation win, when does int4 hurt too much, and what is the cost on a real Linux box vs Colab.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_synthesis.md with a 4-column trade-off table (method, cost-to-train, peak memory at inference, quality drop) and a 1-paragraph 'if you are on a Mac, do X' recommendation at the end.

deliverable_prompts.question: When does each distillation method (int8, LoRA, logit KD, int4) beat the others on throughput?

expected_answers.response: Right - int8 wins on memory-bound workloads (LLM decode, large context) because the weight-load bandwidth drops 4x.
Right - LoRA wins on adaptation cost: cheap fine-tunes for new domains without retraining the full model.

common_misconceptions.response: Not quite - int8 helps memory-bound workloads but can hurt compute-bound ones through dequant overhead. It's not universally faster.

deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good - the 4 methods trade off across memory, compute, adaptation, and quality. Each has a workload class where it's best.
deliverable_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about which bottleneck each method attacks. int8 -> memory bandwidth, LoRA -> adaptation, logit KD -> quality, int4 -> extreme memory pressure.

cuda.title: CUDA Track
cuda.description: Kernels, profiling, memory hierarchy, and GPU execution.

llm.title: LLM Track
llm.description: Scaling laws, training dynamics, inference, and evaluation.

systems.title: Systems Track
systems.description: Inference pipelines, VAD, streaming, serving, and reliability.

distillation.title: Distillation Track
distillation.description: Compression, teacher-student training, quantization, and evaluation.

resources.title: Google Colab
resources.good_for: PyTorch, Transformers, small CUDA experiments, quick GPU time
resources.not_good_for: vLLM serving (long-running), anything needing persistent storage or >1 GPU
resources.notes: Use the GPU runtime. Free-tier availability varies; paid tier is reliable.

resources.title: Kaggle Notebooks
resources.good_for: Free notebook GPU, PyTorch, Transformers, small fine-tuning runs
resources.not_good_for: Anything needing internet at runtime, vLLM serving, anything that needs >1 GPU
resources.notes: 30h/week free GPU. Internet/package install limits apply.

resources.title: Unsloth
resources.good_for: LLM fine-tuning, LoRA, 4-bit / 8-bit quantization, runs in Colab
resources.not_good_for: Raw CUDA kernel work, anything that needs vLLM serving
resources.notes: Best fit for Week 3 quantization and any future distillation weeks. Not for Week 1/2 raw CUDA.

resources.title: RunPod
resources.good_for: Triton, vLLM, real Linux NVIDIA environment, persistent dev box, on-demand pricing
resources.not_good_for: Anything that needs zero setup (you are spinning up a VM)
resources.notes: Best match for Week 2-4 full labs. Per-hour billing; Community Cloud is cheap.

resources.title: Lambda Labs
resources.good_for: Persistent Linux GPU box, multi-GPU training, sustained workloads, full labs
resources.not_good_for: Anything you only need for an hour (use RunPod)
resources.notes: Reserved instances; per-month billing. Good when you know you want a real dev machine for a few weeks.
